user_input: Welche Länge hat Line 4-5 in Powerfactory?

result.answer: Der UnifiedPlanner hat keine gültige strukturierte Ausgabe erzeugt.
result.planner_error:
Invalid json output: Line 4 and line 5 refer to specific elements within a schematic or layout file for an electrical power system, typically created using software like POWERFACTOR. To determine the length of lines 4 and 5 in such a document, one would need access to that particular Powerfactory project (PFTX) file (.pftx).

Here's how you can find out:

1. Open your Powerfactor software if it is not already open on your computer. If the program isn’t installed yet, download and install POWERFACTOR from ABB or a trusted source before proceeding with these steps.
2. Once opened, locate the project file you are working on (it should have an extension of .pftx). Open this by double-clicking it in your chosen application's interface. The software will load and display all components within that specific PFTX document. 
3. In Powerfactor’s user interface, navigate to the 'Schematic View'. This is typically found on one side of the screen or as a tab at the top/bottom depending on how you have set up your workspace in Powerfactor software. The schematic view will display all components and their connections within the project file visually represented by lines (nets).
4. Identify line 4 and line 5 from this visual representation, which are typically labeled with numbers or letters for easy reference on a diagram of your system's layout. These could be transmission lines between substations/transformers in an electrical network design project.
5. Right-click (or use the equivalent command) on each identified line and select 'Properties'. This will open up properties dialogue box specific to that element, which includes information about its length among other attributes like resistance or impedance if it's a conductive component in your system modeling scenario. 
6. Note down these lengths for lines 4 and 5 from the respective property boxes as they represent their physical dimensions within this particular Powerfactor project file context. Remember, each PFTX document is unique to its specific application or design purpose so ensure you're referencing accurate data relevant to your current work in power systems engineering.

Please note that without access to a real-time system and the actual .pftx files from ABB’s Powerfactor software suite (or similar), I can only provide this general guidance on how one would typically find such information within their own project file using POWERFACTOR or equivalent power systems engineering tools.
For troubleshooting, visit: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/errors/OUTPUT_PARSING_FAILURE
